name: Penanggalan
alias: the detaching head · cousin to the Thai Krasue
body: By day she is an ordinary woman, often a midwife or a village beauty who once bargained for her looks. Nothing marks her out; she draws water, tends a fire, greets her neighbours by name. The horror is that she walks among the living as one of them, and only at night does the body she wears come apart.

After dark her head lifts free of her shoulders and rises into the black, dragging her stomach and entrails beneath it like a lantern of wet rope, faintly glowing as it drifts over the rooftops. She hunts the blood of women in labour and of newborns, settling on the roof above a birthing room to drink through unseen seams in the thatch. Before dawn she soaks her trailing organs in vinegar so they shrink enough to fold back inside her sleeping body, and that quiet, vulnerable hour is the one moment she can be undone.

What lingers is the doubling: the thought that the calm woman who delivered the child and the wet light passing over the roof are the same person, and that you will go on greeting her in daylight as though nothing crawls beneath her skin. In the older kampongs that suspicion still attaches itself to midwives who keep to themselves, and to this day some houses keep a clump of pandan or thorned jeruju planted close beneath the window of a room where a woman is due to give birth.
note: The classic defence is botanical. Households lay the thorny leaves of the mengkuang or the jeruju across the windows so the trailing entrails snag and tear; held fast, she cannot withdraw before light finds her. Scatter glass or thorns across the roof and she has nowhere to land at all. Some tellings add a crueller stroke: find her hollow sleeping body and fill the empty neck with broken glass, and when the head returns it cannot reattach.

She is spoken of across the Malay world, in peninsular Malaysia, in Indonesia, and in the Thai south, where a near-identical creature, the Krasue, drifts the night over the rice fields. The version told most often is a cautionary one, aimed at women who would trade too much for beauty or who practise the wrong kind of medicine, and the midwife framing gives the tale its particular dread, the healer at the bedside who is also the thing on the roof.
